claims.text: In 2006 a New York woman named Jeie reported to Dr. Brian Stone a recurring dream featuring an unknown man.

evidence.quote:
in 2006 jeie a woman living in New
York was at her weekly session with her
psychiatrist Dr Brian Stone

claims.rationale: This is the origin-story frame of the This Man legend. Host later confirms the whole narrative is a hoax created by advertising executive Andrea Natella — the New York psychiatrist and patient are invented characters.

claims.text: At least 2,000 people around the world have reported seeing This Man in their dreams.

evidence.quote:
this man had been seen at least 2,000
times

claims.rationale: The 2,000+ figure comes from the thisman.org site which is itself the hoax. Some people plausibly did report sightings after seeing the highly suggestive poster/image, but the headline number is inflated marketing material.

claims.text: This Man was a viral marketing hoax created by Italian advertising executive Andrea Natella to test whether he could manufacture an urban legend.

evidence.quote:
Not only was this man a hoax it was
debunked

the this man myth was an advertising
executive

Nutella wanted to see if he
could create an urban legend

claims.rationale: Andrea Natella is a real Italian guerrilla-marketing executive who publicly admitted in 2010 (confirmed in Vice coverage) that Ever Dream This Man was a fabricated internet-legend experiment. Strong directTruth.

dependencies.rationale: [debunks] The 2006 NYC psychiatrist framing is fictional — the whole This Man phenomenon was a marketing creation.
[undercuts] The 2,000-sightings figure comes from the hoax site itself.

claims.text: The This Man sketch was based on Natella's own father as a young man.

evidence.quote:
a sketch based on his
father as a young man

claims.rationale: Natella has publicly said this in multiple interviews. While unverifiable independently, it is consistent with the overall hoax narrative and is the only explanation he has offered for the specific likeness.

claims.text: Vice initially reported on This Man in January 2015 and had to publish a same-day apology after the hoax was pointed out.

evidence.quote:
Not only was this man a hoax it was
debunked

claims.rationale: Vice did run an Ever Dream This Man piece in January 2015 followed by a correction note — both are archived. The speed of the retraction indicates the hoax was already known to at least some readers.

dependencies.rationale: Vice's retraction is one of the public confirmations that the whole story was fabricated.

claims.text: The CIA ran a sub-project within Stargate on lucid dreaming beginning in 1991, predating the 2010 film Inception by 20 years.

evidence.quote:
project
Stargate where the CIA was using
psychics

CIA had a sub project within
Stargate lucid dreaming

claims.rationale: Project Stargate and SRI's remote-viewing program are declassified and documented. A specific 'lucid dreaming sub-project in 1991' is not in the public declassified Stargate record to my knowledge; this appears to be speculative host embellishment of a real program. Inception's 2010 release date is real.

claims.text: Abraham Lincoln dreamt of his own death shortly before his April 14, 1865 assassination.
claims.rationale: The Ward Hill Lamon anecdote of Lincoln's death dream is genuinely in the historical record (Lamon's 1895 memoirs), but Lamon was an unreliable narrator with a book to sell and no independent corroboration exists. The story is repeated but partially apocryphal.

claims.text: Backup singer JoJo Billingsley dreamt of a plane crash and refused to board the Lynyrd Skynyrd flight that crashed near Gillsburg, Mississippi.
claims.rationale: The October 20, 1977 Lynyrd Skynyrd crash is verifiable. Billingsley did publicly claim a premonition dream prompted her to skip the flight; this story is documented in band biographies but relies on her personal account after the fact — a retrospective premonition claim is hard to falsify.

claims.text: Ten-year-old Eryl Mai Jones predicted the Aberfan disaster in a dream, describing the school buried in black matter.
claims.rationale: The October 1966 Aberfan disaster and Eryl Mai Jones's death are historical fact. Jones did reportedly tell her mother about a nightmare before the tragedy — this is part of the British Premonitions Bureau record. However the story is inherently non-falsifiable: the dream was only reported after the disaster and selection bias dominates premonition cases.

claims.text: Psychiatrist John Barker founded the British Premonitions Bureau to document precognitive dreams after investigating Aberfan.
claims.rationale: John Barker (1924-1968) was a real British psychiatrist who did found the Premonitions Bureau in 1967 following Aberfan. Documented in Sam Knight's book The Premonitions Bureau (2022). Checkable historical fact.

claims.text: Alan Hencher predicted a 1967 Britannia passenger aircraft crash near Cyprus, matching the death toll almost exactly.
claims.rationale: Hencher's prediction and the Britannia Airways flight 105 crash (April 1967, Nicosia) are real entries in Barker's Premonitions Bureau log. Close numerical match of a prediction to a real event of that era is the strongest single case in parapsychology, but still subject to selection bias — Hencher's many failed predictions are less remembered.

dependencies.rationale: Hencher was one of Barker's two star 'percipients' at the Premonitions Bureau.

claims.text: Kathleen Middleton predicted Robert Kennedy's June 1968 assassination but Barker failed to pass the warning on.
claims.rationale: Middleton was a real Premonitions Bureau percipient; Barker's records show she logged multiple predictions. The specific June 1968 RFK call with Barker's inaction is in the Premonitions Bureau history. But the nine-point agreement with the event is generous — RFK was a high-profile public figure and vague 'life in danger' warnings happen constantly.

dependencies.rationale: Middleton was Barker's other star percipient at the Bureau.

claims.text: Sigmund Freud's book The Interpretation of Dreams is considered the foundation of psychoanalysis.

evidence.quote:
Sigman Freud wrote a book
called the interpretation of Dreams

claims.rationale: Freud's Die Traumdeutung was published November 1899 (dated 1900) and is universally recognized as a foundational text of psychoanalysis. Standard intellectual history.

claims.text: Even though This Man is a hoax, suggestion is powerful enough that many of the 8,000 reported sightings were sincere.

evidence.quote:
8,000 people saying they saw him in
their dreams

claims.rationale: Host's closing nuance — suggestion and priming effects are well-documented in dream research; once someone has seen the widely-distributed poster image, incorporating it into a dream is plausible without requiring deception or coincidence.

dependencies.rationale: Completes the debunking story: hoax origin plus genuine psychological mechanism for the spread of reports.
